title: EHRC Technical Guidance: Sexual Harassment and Harassment at Work
effectiveDate: January 2020 (updated October 2024 for Worker Protection Act 2023)
chapters: Technical Guidance · Originally published January 2020 · Updated October 2024 for Worker Protection Act 2023 (new s.40A preventive duty) · Covers sexual harassment, harassment related to sex, and the employer's preventive duty
desc: The EHRC Technical Guidance on Sexual Harassment and Harassment at Work provides comprehensive guidance on the legal framework for preventing and responding to sexual harassment in the workplace. Originally published in January 2020 following the Women and Equalities Select Committee's inquiry into sexual harassment in the workplace, the guidance was updated in October 2024 to incorporate the Worker Protection (Amendment of Equality Act 2010) Act 2023, which introduced the new proactive duty on employers to take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment (new EqA 2010 s.40A, in force 26 October 2024). The guidance covers: the legal definition of sexual harassment (EqA 2010 s.26(2) — unwanted conduct of a sexual nature), the distinction between sexual harassment and harassment related to sex, the employer's new preventive duty under s.40A, what constitutes 'reasonable steps' (risk assessment, training, reporting channels, swift action), the 25% compensation uplift for breach of the preventive duty, the employer's vicarious liability and 'all reasonable steps' defence, the role of NDAs and confidentiality agreements in harassment cases, and the anticipated ERA 2025 strengthening from 'reasonable steps' to 'all reasonable steps'. The ERA 2025 will also introduce a new obligation on employers not to permit harassment of employees by third parties.
legalStatus: Technical guidance — not a statutory code. However, tribunals regularly refer to EHRC technical guidance as authoritative. The guidance on reasonable steps for the s.40A preventive duty is likely to be treated as the benchmark for compliance.

parts.title: Legal Framework and the Preventive Duty

sections.title: Sexual harassment definitions and the new preventive duty
sections.summary: Sexual harassment under EqA 2010 s.26(2) is unwanted conduct of a sexual nature which has the purpose or effect of violating the individual's dignity, or creating an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating or offensive environment. It is distinct from harassment related to sex (s.26(1) — unwanted conduct related to the protected characteristic of sex). There is also a third form: less favourable treatment because of rejection of, or submission to, sexual harassment or harassment related to sex (s.26(3)). The new preventive duty under s.40A (in force 26 October 2024) requires employers to take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment of employees in the course of employment. It is not a standalone claim — it operates as a compensation uplift (up to 25%) where an underlying sexual harassment claim succeeds and the tribunal finds the employer failed to take reasonable steps. The EHRC guidance recommends: carrying out sexual harassment risk assessments, providing regular training, establishing clear reporting channels, taking prompt action on complaints, and monitoring effectiveness. ERA 2025 will strengthen the duty from 'reasonable steps' to 'all reasonable steps' and introduce third-party harassment liability.
sections.text:
EqA 2010 s.26 — Three types of harassment:

(1) Harassment related to a relevant protected characteristic: unwanted conduct related to the characteristic that has the purpose or effect of violating dignity or creating a hostile environment.

(2) Sexual harassment: unwanted conduct of a sexual nature that has the same purpose or effect.

(3) Less favourable treatment because of rejection or submission: treating a person less favourably because they rejected or submitted to (1) or (2).

EqA 2010 s.40A — Preventive duty (from 26 October 2024):
• Employers must take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment of employees in the course of employment
• What constitutes 'reasonable steps' depends on the employer's size, resources, sector, working environment, and known risks
• Breach: if a sexual harassment claim succeeds and the tribunal finds the employer did not take reasonable steps, compensation may be uplifted by up to 25%
• Not a standalone claim — requires an underlying s.26(2) claim to succeed

EHRC recommended reasonable steps:
• Risk assessment: identify scenarios where sexual harassment may occur (considering all worker types — employees, agency workers, volunteers, interns)
• Policy: clear anti-harassment policy communicated to all staff
• Training: regular, effective, refreshed training — not a one-off exercise
• Reporting channels: multiple accessible routes for raising complaints
• Prompt action: swift, proportionate response to complaints
• Monitoring: regular review of the effectiveness of preventive measures
• Third-party risks: assess risks from customers, clients, service users, contractors, and members of the public

versions.description: Original technical guidance published following the Women and Equalities Select Committee inquiry (2018).
Guidance updated to incorporate the Worker Protection (Amendment of Equality Act 2010) Act 2023 — new s.40A preventive duty. Detailed guidance on 'reasonable steps' published alongside commencement.

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 strengthens the preventive duty from 'reasonable steps' to 'all reasonable steps' (expected October 2026). Also introduces a new obligation on employers not to permit harassment of employees by third parties — reinstating the third-party harassment liability that was repealed by ERRA 2013 s.65.
pendingAmendment.commencementStatus: Enacted. Not yet in force. Expected October 2026.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: Discrimination category. The move from 'reasonable steps' to 'all reasonable steps' raises the compliance bar significantly. Third-party harassment liability reintroduces an obligation repealed in 2013. FM sector employers whose staff interact with members of the public and client employees are directly affected.

sections.eraNote: ERA 2025 will strengthen the s.40A duty from 'reasonable steps' to 'all reasonable steps' and introduce third-party harassment liability. Forward Intelligence classification: HIGH (Royal Assent achieved, expected October 2026).

leadingCases.name: Pemberton v Inwood [2018] EWCA Civ 564
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed the two-stage test for harassment: (1) was the conduct unwanted and related to a protected characteristic (or of a sexual nature)? (2) did it have the purpose or effect of violating dignity or creating a hostile environment? For the 'effect' limb, the tribunal must consider the claimant's perception, the other circumstances, and whether it is reasonable for the conduct to have that effect.
leadingCases.significance: Definitive authority on the two-stage harassment test including the reasonableness requirement for the 'effect' limb

leadingCases.name: Raj v Capita Business Services Ltd [2019] UKEAT/0074/19
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that for conduct to be 'related to' a protected characteristic, there must be a factual connection between the conduct and the characteristic. The conduct does not need to be motivated by the characteristic — the question is whether the conduct has a connection to it.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified the 'related to' requirement — broader than 'because of' but still requires a factual connection

refs: Worker Protection (Amendment of Equality Act 2010) Act 2023
ERA 2025 (strengthening to 'all reasonable steps' + third-party harassment — expected October 2026)
EqA 2010 s.109 (employer vicarious liability) and s.109(4) ('all reasonable steps' defence)
Allay (UK) Ltd v Gehlen [2021] (stale training insufficient)

pendingAmendments: EqA 2010 s.40A preventive duty IN FORCE 26 October 2024
ERA 2025 — 'all reasonable steps' replacing 'reasonable steps' (expected October 2026)
ERA 2025 s.94 — third-party harassment liability reinstatement (expected October 2026)
EHRC enforcement powers under s.40A — monitoring for first enforcement actions

parliamentaryOrigin.statutoryBasis: Technical Guidance — not a statutory code, but treated as authoritative by tribunals. Published under EHRC's general guidance function (Equality Act 2006 s.13).
parliamentaryOrigin.currentEdition: January 2020 (updated October 2024 for Worker Protection Act 2023)
parliamentaryOrigin.legalEffect: Tribunals regularly refer to EHRC technical guidance as authoritative. The s.40A preventive duty guidance is the benchmark for compliance assessment. The guidance defines what constitutes 'reasonable steps' for the purposes of s.40A and the employer's vicarious liability defence under s.109(4).
parliamentaryOrigin.note: Published following the Women and Equalities Select Committee's 2018 inquiry into sexual harassment in the workplace. Updated October 2024 for the new s.40A preventive duty. ERA 2025 will further strengthen from 'reasonable steps' to 'all reasonable steps' and reinstate third-party harassment liability.

precedingDocuments.title: Women and Equalities Select Committee: Sexual Harassment in the Workplace (5th Report, 2017-19)
precedingDocuments.significance: The inquiry that catalysed the EHRC guidance, the Worker Protection Act 2023, and ultimately the ERA 2025 harassment provisions.

precedingDocuments.title: Worker Protection (Amendment of Equality Act 2010) Act 2023
precedingDocuments.significance: Inserted EqA 2010 s.40A — preventive duty for sexual harassment. In force 26 October 2024.

leadingCases.name: Pemberton v Inwood [2018] EWCA Civ 564
leadingCases.heldText: Two-stage harassment test: (1) unwanted conduct of sexual nature; (2) purpose or effect of violating dignity / creating hostile environment. Reasonableness check on the 'effect' limb.

leadingCases.name: Allay (UK) v Gehlen [2021] ICR 645
leadingCases.heldText: Stale training is not a reasonable step. Training must be current, refreshed, and effective.
leadingCases.significance: Directly relevant to s.40A compliance — stale training will not satisfy the preventive duty.

leadingCases.name: Unite the Union v Nailard [2018] EWCA Civ 1203
leadingCases.heldText: Following ERRA 2013 repeal of EqA s.40(2)-(4), remaining provisions cannot create employer liability for third-party harassment. ERA 2025 s.94 will reinstate this.
leadingCases.significance: The gap ERA 2025 s.94 will close — third-party harassment liability.
